ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn entry covers halibut as a food ingredient; no specific entry for Chinese-style steamed halibut dish exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers halibut as a raw commodity. No specific code for steamed halibut dish; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code covers halibut as a substance/ingredient. No specific code for the prepared steamed dish.

preparation_method: seasoned with soy sauce and ginger
garnished with spring onion and sesame oil

serving.description: 1 portion (approximately half a fish or a fillet portion)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of steamed halibut is approximately 250 g, representing a fillet or half-fish portion served with soy-ginger sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Halibut, Atlantic and Pacific, cooked, dry heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175136)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy values for cooked halibut (dry heat) used as the primary nutrient basis. Steaming is a comparable low-fat cooking method to dry heat for white fish; macronutrient profile is closely analogous. Sodium adjusted upward to approximately 350 mg per 100 g to account for soy sauce seasoning typical of Chinese-style steamed fish preparation. All other macros retained from USDA cooked halibut values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: Halibut contains negligible carbohydrate (0 g per 100 g cooked). Pure protein-and-fat foods such as fish and seafood are universally classified as very low GI (estimated GI ~5) by the University of Sydney GI Database convention for non-carbohydrate foods. Glycemic load per 250 g serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g is 0. GI set to 5 as the lowest defensible positive estimate for a protein-dominant food with no glycemic carbohydrates.

hypertension.guidance: Chinese-style steamed halibut is seasoned with soy sauce, which can contribute approximately 350 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium soy sauce or limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content and high-quality protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Steamed halibut contains essentially zero carbohydrate and has a glycemic index of approximately 5, making it an excellent protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes who need to manage postprandial blood glucose; it supports satiety without raising blood sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids and low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Halibut provides beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and is low in saturated fat (0.33 g per 100 g), supporting cardiovascular health; steaming avoids added cooking fats, and regular consumption of white fish is associated with reduced cardiovascular risk per AHA dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Halibut (清蒸比目鱼, Qīng zhēng bǐ mù yú) is a classic Chinese seafood dish featuring halibut — a large, mild-flavoured flatfish — prepared by steaming over high heat and finished with hot soy sauce, julienned ginger, spring onion, and a drizzle of sesame oil. The dish is a staple of Cantonese and Chinese seafood cuisine and is widely served in Singapore seafood restaurants. Halibut has a firm yet delicate white flesh with a clean, subtly sweet flavour and a moist, flaky texture when properly steamed. Nutritionally, steamed halibut is an excellent source of high-quality complete protein (approximately 22.5 g per 100 g), very low in fat (2.3 g per 100 g), and contains zero carbohydrate, making it highly suitable for low-carbohydrate, diabetic-friendly, and weight-management diets. It is rich in selenium, vitamin D, vitamin B12, niacin, potassium, magnesium, and phosphorus. The primary clinical consideration is sodium from soy sauce seasoning, which can be moderated by requesting low-sodium soy sauce. The dish is naturally gluten-free if tamari is used instead of regular soy sauce. It is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and those seeking lean protein sources. In Singapore, it is commonly served at Chinese seafood restaurants and zi char stalls as a shared dish alongside rice.
common_user_queries: How many calories in steamed halibut?
What is 比目鱼 in English?
Is steamed fish good for high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked halibut (FDC ID 175136), which is a high-quality approved source for the fish itself. Sodium has been adjusted upward to reflect soy sauce seasoning typical of Chinese-style steamed fish. No Singapore HPB Foodo entry for steamed halibut was identified. GI is an analogous estimate based on the universal convention for non-carbohydrate protein foods. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the sodium adaptation and absence of an exact Singapore-specific source.
notes: 1. carbohydrate_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked halibut; halibut is a pure protein-and-fat food with naturally zero carbohydrate content. 2. sugar_g is 0.0: naturally zero as halibut contains no sugars. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: naturally zero as halibut is an animal-derived food with no dietary fibre. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked halibut. 5. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; fish does not contain vitamin C. 6. Sodium value of 350 mg per 100 g is an adaptation from the USDA base value of approximately 69 mg per 100 g for plain cooked halibut, adjusted to reflect soy sauce seasoning in Chinese-style preparation; this is a conservative estimate and actual sodium will vary by restaurant. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.75), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.60), potassium_mg (576.0), calcium_mg (60.0), iron_mg (1.07), cholesterol_mg (46.0), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (47.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.06), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.08), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (6.05), vitamin_b6_mg (0.45), vitamin_b12_mcg (1.16), folate_mcg_dfe (12.0), vitamin_d_mcg (4.9), vitamin_e_mg (0.62), phosphorus_mg (285.0), magnesium_mg (107.0), zinc_mg (0.53), selenium_mcg (46.8), water_g (72.9) are all sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175136 for cooked halibut. 8. GI set to 5 as the lowest defensible positive estimate for a protein-dominant food with no glycemic carbohydrates; glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g is 0. 9. The input cuisine_tags included 'Crab' and 'BBQ' which appear to be platform-level tags for the restaurant category rather than descriptors of this specific halibut dish; these have not been carried forward. 10. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03411433 is used as closest parent for halibut as a food; no exact entry for Chinese steamed halibut dish exists in FoodOn. 11. FoodEx2 code A00LC is used as closest parent for halibut commodity; no dish-level code exists.
